takeaways: Senior housing occupancy is approaching 90% nationally with construction starts at historic lows — existing operators like Lloyd Jones are positioned to benefit from widening supply-demand gaps through 2028.

CRE capital markets are reopening with commercial mortgage rates starting at 5.38% and Colliers forecasting a 15–20% jump in sales activity, but tariff uncertainty and 'decaf stagflation' keep investors selective.
Proptech funding surged 176% in January 2026 alone as agentic AI moves from pilot to production — senior housing operators who adopt AI-driven property management could slash ticket volume by 25–40%.

items.headline: Senior Living Occupancy Surpasses 89%, Headed Toward 90% in 2026
items.summary: NIC MAP data shows senior living occupancy hit 89%+ across 31 primary markets, marking the 17th consecutive quarter of improvement. Independent living topped 90% for the first time since 2019. With construction starts at their lowest since 2009 and a 29-month average build cycle, new supply won't meaningfully arrive until 2028 — giving existing operators significant pricing power.

items.headline: Mainstay CIO: Middle-Market Senior Living Is a Math Problem
items.summary: Mainstay's CIO argues that serving middle-income seniors in secondary and tertiary Southeast markets is fundamentally an affordability equation, not a branding exercise. As Boomers delay entry until they want lifestyle benefits rather than care, demand for detached living options is intensifying — a segment that remains critically underserved.

items.headline: Construction Lags Underscore Need for New Senior Living Development
items.summary: New units breaking ground have fallen below the number coming online — a pattern last seen during the 2009 financial crisis. Even if starts accelerate now, the 29-month construction cycle means projects won't open before 2028. The supply gap creates both opportunity for developers who can secure financing and risk for markets facing acute demand.

items.headline: Leo Brown Finishes Florida Community; United Properties Starts Minnesota Project
items.summary: Leo Brown Group completed a new senior living community in Florida while United Properties broke ground on Amira Corcoran, an active adult community in Corcoran, Minnesota. These projects reflect continued but selective development activity in markets with strong demographic tailwinds.

items.headline: CRE Mortgage Rates Start at 5.38% as Fed Holds at 3.50–3.75%
items.summary: As of April 2, 2026, commercial mortgage rates range from 4.99% to 12.75% depending on property type, with multifamily loans over $6M at 5.16% and retail at 6.07%. The Fed held rates steady at 3.50–3.75% in January, with the 10-Year Treasury stabilized around 4.00–4.25%. Senior housing operators refinancing or acquiring should find improved but still selective lending conditions.

items.headline: Colliers Forecasts 15–20% Jump in CRE Sales Activity for 2026
items.summary: Institutional and cross-border capital is returning to U.S. commercial real estate, with Colliers projecting a 15–20% increase in transaction volume this year. Newmark's base case is 'decaf stagflation' — below-trend growth with stubborn inflation — which supports gradual improvement but limits aggressive rate cuts.

items.headline: Tariff Uncertainty Delays CRE Investment Decisions Across Asset Classes
items.summary: With the average tariff rate at 12% on all imports, elevated material costs and policy uncertainty are causing tenants and investors to delay leasing and acquisition decisions. The Supreme Court ruled Trump's reciprocal tariffs unconstitutional in February 2026, but ongoing trade policy volatility continues to weigh on capital expenditure planning for projects ranging from $50M to $300M.

items.headline: Greystone: Senior Housing Supply Constraints Drive Capital Momentum
items.summary: Greystone's 2026 outlook highlights the convergence of supply constraints, rising occupancy, and renewed capital flows into senior housing. The firm sees integrated care models and middle-market affordability as key themes shaping investment strategy for the sector.

items.headline: Proptech Funding Surges 176% in January as Agentic AI Takes Center Stage
items.summary: Venture capital firms invested $1.7 billion in proptech in January 2026 alone, a 176% increase year-over-year. Full-year 2025 proptech investment hit $16.7 billion globally. Agentic AI — autonomous systems that execute decisions, not just generate content — is the dominant investment thesis, with applications spanning procurement, pricing, and property management.

items.headline: Agentic AI Reshaping Retail and Real Estate Operations at Scale
items.summary: ICSC reports that agentic AI is moving beyond chatbots to autonomous decision-making in real estate. Retailers and operators are deploying AI agents for procurement, dynamic pricing, inventory management, and event programming. For senior housing operators, AI-driven property management can cut maintenance ticket volume by 25–40% and first-response time from hours to minutes.

items.headline: Four New Proptech Unicorns Highlight AI's Growing CRE Role
items.summary: Bisnow reports on four proptech companies reaching unicorn status, led by EliseAI ($2.2B valuation, $250M Series E) and Bedrock Robotics ($1.75B, $270M raise in February 2026). Smart Bricks raised $5M from Andreessen Horowitz for AI-native real estate underwriting. AI-centered proptech investment is growing at 42% annually, nearly double the rate of non-AI companies.

items.headline: 92% of CRE Companies Now Running AI Pilots, Up from 5% Three Years Ago
items.summary: The number of commercial real estate firms running AI pilots has exploded from 5% to 92% in just three years. The proptech market reached $54.66 billion in 2026, on track for $185 billion by 2034. For operators, the shift from experimentation to production means ROI expectations are tightening — tools must deliver measurable efficiency gains to survive.

items.headline: DDI: 71% of Leaders Under Increased Stress, 40% Consider Leaving
items.summary: DDI's Global Leadership Forecast reveals a leadership crisis: 71% of leaders report increased stress and 40% are considering leaving their roles. Meanwhile, 77% of CHROs lack confidence in their leadership bench strength. For senior living operators managing high-turnover environments, investing in leadership development and succession planning is increasingly urgent.

items.headline: Human-AI Leadership Emerges as Top Executive Competency for 2026
items.summary: The World Economic Forum and multiple leadership studies identify human + AI leadership as the defining executive capability of 2026. As AI embeds in daily workflows, differentiation comes from culture — how teams collaborate, make decisions under pressure, and stay engaged. Emotional intelligence is now the critical capacity that enables technology adoption.

items.headline: WEF: Analytical Thinking and Resilience Are Top Executive Skills
items.summary: The World Economic Forum's Future of Jobs Report highlights analytical thinking, resilience, flexibility, agility, and social influence as the most valuable executive skills through 2030. Organizations that learn fastest when strategies break — not those with the most polished plans — will outperform. Flatter organizational structures and adaptive leadership are replacing rigid hierarchies.

items.headline: Sticker Shock: Senior Living Operators Tackle Affordability Perception
items.summary: Provision Living and Asbury are addressing prospects' affordability concerns and decision paralysis head-on with new pricing transparency and value communication strategies. As private-pay senior living grapples with sticker shock in 2026, operators who demystify costs and demonstrate ROI on lifestyle benefits are converting more prospects.

items.headline: 89% of Employees Perform Better When Prioritizing Wellness
items.summary: Wellhub's 2026 State of Work-Life Wellness Report, surveying 5,000+ employees across 10 countries, found that 89% perform better when they prioritize health through structured programs. Companies with wellness programs see 61% of employees reporting good or thriving wellbeing vs. 40% without. Every dollar spent returns $1.47 in productivity gains.

items.headline: 90% of Workers Experienced Burnout Symptoms in Past Year
items.summary: Across the workforce, 90% of employees experienced burnout symptoms in the past year, with nearly 40% experiencing them weekly. In response, 41% of employers plan to increase wellness spending in the next 1–2 years, prioritizing mental health, preventive physical health, and financial wellness. For senior living operators managing caregiver burnout, these trends directly impact retention.

items.headline: Global Wellness Summit: Emotional Fitness Is the New Health Category
items.summary: The Global Wellness Summit identified emotional fitness as a breakout wellness category for 2026, alongside biomarker-based nutrition and AI wellness coaching. Wellness is becoming hyper-personal and data-driven, with smart wearables and AI platforms delivering customized health recommendations. The wellness economy continues expanding as prevention replaces reaction.

items.headline: Companies With Wellness Programs See 20% Higher Productivity
items.summary: The Global Wellness Institute reports that companies integrating well-being into leadership and culture achieve up to 20% higher productivity. Gyms, wellness studios, and outdoor spaces are emerging as the new 'third places' where employees recharge and connect — a trend senior living communities can leverage for both resident programming and staff retention.
